skill.name: onboarding-helper
skill.description: Generate structured onboarding guides, setup checklists, learning paths, and team process documentation for new developers.
skill.summary: Create developer onboarding documentation that explains setup, architecture, first tasks, and team workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many command, network, environment, and credential-related patterns, but they appear inside documentation templates rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. Publication should include a warning because the template normalizes unsafe setup examples such as pipe-to-shell installation, local secret placeholders, browser token storage, and credential lookup instructions.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pipe-to-Shell Installation Example
medium_findings.description: The onboarding template recommends installing tools by piping remote curl output directly into bash. This is not malicious skill code, but generated onboarding docs could cause users to run unaudited remote scripts.
medium_findings.recommendation: Replace pipe-to-shell installation examples with package-manager or checksum-verified installation steps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly shown in an onboarding setup template and combines network retrieval with shell execution. It is documentation, not executable skill behavior, so medium risk is more accurate than critical.

medium_findings.title: Secret and Environment Variable Handling in Generated Docs
medium_findings.description: The template includes local environment files, database URLs, API keys, AWS keys, session secrets, and references to a password vault. These are placeholders, but they can encourage unsafe copying of secrets into onboarding documents.
medium_findings.recommendation: Keep real secrets out of generated output and direct users to approved secret-management workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly discuss sensitive credential categories and storage locations. The values are examples or placeholders, so this is a documentation safety issue rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Browser Storage Used for Tokens and User Activity
medium_findings.description: Example code stores authentication tokens and recently viewed product data in localStorage. This can normalize insecure token storage and client-side persistence patterns in generated onboarding examples.
medium_findings.recommendation: Add guidance to prefer secure, httpOnly cookie-based sessions for tokens and to review browser storage for privacy requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly use localStorage for token and activity data. The risk is contextual because these are teaching snippets in documentation, not active exfiltration code.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Are Mostly Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are common development workflow examples such as git, npm, docker, tests, and local debugging commands. They are not executed by the skill and do not show user-controlled command construction.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep command examples project-specific, reviewed, and limited to necessary setup or verification steps.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is clearly markdown documentation templates and FAQ examples. I found no code path where the skill itself executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Example Resources
low_findings.description: Network findings point to public documentation, example application URLs, localhost endpoints, and placeholder service URLs used in onboarding templates. No data transmission or beaconing behavior was found.
low_findings.recommendation: Review generated links before publishing and avoid private URLs unless access is intended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in markdown resources and setup examples. They do not appear in executable network request code inside the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance findings appear to be broad pattern matches against documentation terms, markdown filenames, setup troubleshooting, or ordinary developer tooling examples.
low_findings.recommendation: Do not treat these static matches as blockers without additional evidence of malicious collection or scanning behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I reviewed representative regions and found documentation examples rather than cryptographic implementation or reconnaissance logic. The confidence is lower because not every listed static line was individually expanded.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Download Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: A sample setup step pipes curl output to bash. This is a dangerous pattern even when used as documentation because it teaches new users to execute remote scripts without verification.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use package-manager installation instructions or verified downloads with checksum validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and semantically confirmed in an install section. It remains documentation-only, so it is not evidence of malicious skill execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Placeholders in Environment File Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The template shows environment variables for database, Stripe, AWS, and session secrets in a local .env file. The values are placeholders, but this can lead to unsafe secret handling if copied into generated guides.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use placeholder names only and point to approved secret provisioning steps instead of embedding values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive variable names are clear and located in environment setup instructions. No real secret values were found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Data in Browser Storage Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The template demonstrates storing tokens and recently viewed items in localStorage. This can create security and privacy issues if copied into production onboarding examples.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Add warnings and safer alternatives for authentication token storage and privacy-sensitive browser persistence.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage usage is directly visible in code examples. The risk is educational reuse rather than active data collection.

content.value_statement: New developers often need setup steps, architecture context, and team process details in one clear guide. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft practical onboarding materials from project context.
actual_capabilities: Drafts complete onboarding guides with day one, week one, and month one milestones.
Creates development environment setup sections with prerequisites, install steps, and verification checks.
Organizes architecture overviews, codebase maps, workflow notes, and team process documentation.
Produces first-task tutorials with branch, implementation, test, and pull request steps.
Adds FAQ, troubleshooting, resource lists, and buddy guidance for self-service onboarding.
limitations: The skill provides documentation templates and does not verify commands against a live repository.
Example credentials and URLs must be replaced with organization-approved secure references.
Generated setup commands need review before publication or execution by new hires.
It cannot infer private team culture, access rules, or architecture details without user input.

use_cases.title: Prepare New Engineer Onboarding
use_cases.description: Create a structured guide that explains accounts, setup, milestones, team rituals, and first-week expectations.

use_cases.title: Document Project Setup
use_cases.description: Turn scattered setup notes into a clear sequence with prerequisites, environment variables, verification steps, and troubleshooting.

use_cases.title: Design First Contribution Paths
use_cases.description: Build beginner-friendly first-task walkthroughs that introduce architecture, testing practices, review flow, and ownership boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Onboarding Guide
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small team or project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a developer onboarding guide for this project. Include day one tasks, setup steps, key documentation, team contacts, and a short FAQ.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Setup Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request focused on local development setup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a local development setup checklist for new engineers. Include prerequisites, repository setup, environment variables, verification steps, and common troubleshooting notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build a First-Week Learning Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a first-week learning path for a new developer. Include daily goals, codebase reading, hands-on exercises, review checkpoints, and expected outcomes.

prompt_templates.title: Assemble a Complete Onboarding Program
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a full engineering onboarding package.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a complete onboarding package for a new backend engineer. Cover architecture, setup, access requests, coding standards, first contribution, testing, deployment, on-call expectations, and security review notes.

output_examples.input: Create day one onboarding for a React and Node.js project.
output_examples.output: A day one checklist with account access, tool installation, repository setup, local app verification, team introductions, and links to architecture and contribution guides.

output_examples.input: Help document a first ticket for a new frontend engineer.
output_examples.output: A first-task walkthrough that explains the feature goal, files to inspect, implementation steps, test expectations, manual QA checks, and pull request guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a troubleshooting section for setup failures.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting guide that groups common failures by dependency, database, ports, environment variables, and permissions, with clear owner contacts for escalation.

best_practices: Replace sample commands, URLs, and credentials with reviewed project-specific instructions before sharing.
Ask the skill to include security notes for secrets, token storage, dependency installation, and production access.
Validate generated onboarding materials with a recent hire, a team lead, and the owner of each system.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated setup commands without checking package sources and installation safety.
Do not include real secrets, private URLs, or unrestricted production access steps in onboarding guides.
Do not use the template as a substitute for current repository documentation or access policy review.

faq.question: Does this skill run setup commands?
faq.answer: No. It generates documentation text and examples. Users must review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it create onboarding for any technology stack?
faq.answer: Yes, if you provide the stack, repository structure, setup requirements, and team workflow details.

faq.question: Can it include team culture and process notes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can organize communication norms, meetings, review practices, ownership, and escalation paths.

faq.question: Should I include real credentials in prompts?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders and link to approved secret management processes instead.

faq.question: Can it create first-task tutorials?
faq.answer: Yes. It can outline beginner tasks with files to inspect, steps to follow, tests, and pull request guidance.

faq.question: What review is needed before publishing output?
faq.answer: Review setup commands, access instructions, security guidance, team contacts, and links for accuracy.
